_patch_meta.session_ref: M20 Phase 2 Pass A meaning record via Claude API
_patch_meta.source: Claude API meaning record (Sonnet 4.6); 63 verses across 16 terms

set.analysis_note: A father's anxious concern arises when his son is overdue — the mind projects worst-case outcomes for someone loved, generating inner distress about another's welfare.
A father's anxiety has shifted from the lost donkeys to his missing son — inner worry transfers its object to a person, expressing itself in helpless questioning.
Despair here is strategic rather than emotional — the plan counts on an enemy giving up pursuit, using his expected loss of hope as a tactical advantage.
Disquieting thoughts arise from night visions during deep sleep — the inner person is disturbed by impressions received when conscious defenses are down.
The speech of a despairing man is dismissed as mere wind — despair here empties words of weight, as the sufferer's inner collapse renders his expression valueless to others.
Inner disquiet drives an urgent, hasty reply — restless thoughts within the person compel a response they cannot suppress.
The term here describes God stilling the sea — this is cosmic power over external chaos, not inner-being content for this cluster characteristic.
The verse focuses on confession and sorrow for sin rather than directly evidencing anxiety — the inner state here is one of penitent grief, not anxious dread.
The brokenhearted are the victims of relentless pursuit — their disheartened state results from external cruelty that crushed the inner person to the point of death.
The brokenhearted person is hunted to destruction — their disheartened inner state renders them unable to resist, their spirit already defeated by cruelty.
The psalmist hates the double-minded — those whose inner allegiance is split between God's law and something else — contrasting them with his own wholehearted love of the law.
The heart is deliberately surrendered to despair — the will turns the self over to hopelessness as a conscious response to the futility perceived in all labor.
The term describes God stirring the sea — this is external cosmic action, not inner-being content for this cluster characteristic.
Despair was available but refused — despite exhaustion, the person did not say 'it is hopeless,' finding renewed strength instead; the will resisted surrender to hopelessness.
Anxiety here is dread and fear of another that led to lying and forgetting God — the inner person's fearful orientation toward others displaced reverence and memory of God.
Despair is invoked as a self-justifying declaration — 'it is hopeless' becomes a rationalization for stubborn pursuit of false loves, the will using despair to close off repentance.
set.keywords: ["despair self justifying", "will defiant", "hope rejected", "repentance refused", "desire rationalizing"]

set.analysis_note: The God-rooted person does not become anxious even in drought — deep spiritual grounding neutralizes anxiety, preventing fearful inner distress when circumstances threaten.
Despair ('it is in vain') is used to dismiss any call to change — the declaration of hopelessness cements the will's stubborn commitment to its own evil inclinations.
The term describes God stirring the sea — this is external cosmic action by divine sovereignty, not inner-being content for this cluster characteristic.
The king's anxiety is a specific fear of human enemies — inner dread of personal cruelty at the hands of known opponents paralyzes his will and shapes his decision.
The sword they already fear will pursue them — their anxiety about the threat does not ward it off but will be fulfilled, showing that dread does not protect.
False prophets deliberately disheartened the righteous through lies — their inner spirit of moral courage was crushed by deceitful words, while the wicked were emboldened.
The righteous person's heart was brought low by false prophetic words — their inner moral resolve was broken through deliberate spiritual manipulation.
set.keywords: ["heart broken", "resolve weakened", "spirit disheartened", "deception crushing", "moral courage lost"]

set.analysis_note: Military setback produces fear and withdrawal — the inner person is daunted, and this disheartened state drives a reactive rage and treacherous realignment.
Being afraid and withdrawing signals an inner state of disheartened defeat that then redirects the will toward violent retaliation and betrayal.
Anxiety about basic physical needs is addressed as a posture of the inner life — the command not to be anxious targets a habitual orientation of worry about survival.
Anxiety is exposed as powerless — the inner state of worry cannot change a single external outcome, highlighting its futility as a response to life's uncertainties.
Anxiety about clothing reflects a mind that over-manages the merely material — the question challenges this inner preoccupation by pointing to creation's untroubled provision.
set.keywords: ["anxiety material", "mind over managing", "worry clothing", "trust lacking", "preoccupation inner"]

set.analysis_note: Anxious questioning about basic needs is a pattern of the inner life to be broken — the repetitive 'what shall we' shows worry cycling through the mind without resolution.
Projecting anxiety into tomorrow compounds present trouble — the inner person is called to confine worry to the present day rather than borrowing future distress.
Anxiety about speech under persecution is specifically forbidden — the mind's fearful rehearsal of how to defend itself is to give way to trust in divine provision of words.
Worldly cares function as inner thorns that choke the word — anxious concern about life's circumstances competes with and ultimately smothers spiritual receptivity.
Doubt arises mid-action as the disciple's faith falters while walking on water — the inner wavering between trust and fear produces the physical sinking.
Even at the resurrection appearance, some doubted — their inner faculty of belief remained split, unable to fully commit despite seeing, worship and doubt coexisting.
Worldly cares enter the heart alongside riches and desires and together choke the word — the inner person is overwhelmed by competing concerns that crowd out spiritual growth.
Cares of life choke spiritual growth as the person journeys on — ongoing anxiety about earthly circumstances gradually strangles whatever word had taken root.
Martha's distraction with serving fragments her attention — she is pulled away from the one thing needful by a multiplicity of tasks, her inner focus scattered.
Anxiety and being troubled about many things is named as Martha's inner condition — the mind's scattering across multiple concerns is identified as her spiritual problem.
Anxiety about self-defense before authorities is forbidden — the mind's fearful preparation for persecution is to be displaced by trust in divine provision of speech.
Anxiety about life's basic provisions is directly prohibited — the inner orientation of constant worry about physical sustenance is called out as incompatible with discipleship.
Anxiety is exposed as impotent — the inner straining of worry accomplishes nothing, not even the smallest extension of life, revealing its futility.
Inability to control even small things makes anxiety about greater things irrational — the inner habit of worry is challenged by pointing to the limits of human agency.
set.keywords: ["anxiety irrational", "worry habit", "control limited", "mind over reaching", "trust deficient"]

set.analysis_note: Losing heart is the failure that persistent prayer guards against — the inner person is prone to giving up when prayer seems unanswered, and the parable specifically targets this tendency.
Worldly cares weigh down the heart alongside dissipation and drunkenness — anxious concern about daily life burdens the inner person and dulls readiness for what is coming.
set.keywords: ["care weighing", "heart burdened", "concern dulling", "readiness lost", "soul weighed down"]

set.analysis_note: The unmarried person's anxiety is properly directed toward pleasing the Lord — their undivided concern is oriented to sacred rather than worldly things, making worry itself constructive.
The married man's anxiety is split toward worldly things and his wife — his inner concern is divided between human obligation and divine pleasing.

A married woman's interests are divided between pleasing God and pleasing her husband — inner concern fractures along lines of competing loyalty, contrasting with the undivided devotion of the unmarried.
Mutual care among body members is described as genuine concern for one another — here anxiety is transformed into positive, outward-directed attentiveness rather than self-focused worry.
Affliction so extreme that life itself seemed forfeit produced absolute despair — the inner person was crushed beyond all capacity to cope, all resource for survival apparently gone.
Ministry received through mercy becomes the ground for not losing heart — the inner person resists discouragement by anchoring identity in divine grace rather than personal strength.
Perplexity and pressure press hard on the inner person, yet do not collapse into full despair — the soul is stretched to the edge but not abandoned to hopelessness.
Though the body's decay could drain the will, the inner self is daily renewed, so losing heart is refused — renewal of spirit counters the pull toward collapse.
Daily pastoral responsibility generates a constant internal pressure of anxious concern — an ongoing burden of care carried in the soul for others' wellbeing.
The will is in danger of flagging under sustained effort; losing heart is presented as a real inner risk that must be actively resisted by fixing hope on future harvest.
set.keywords: ["will flagging", "weariness moral", "heart giving up", "endurance required", "hope future"]

set.analysis_note: Witnessing another's suffering on one's behalf creates the inner risk of losing heart; the exhortation reframes that suffering as glory to stabilize the soul against collapse.
Genuine inward concern for others' welfare is presented as a rare quality — an authentic orientation of care directed outward rather than toward self-interest.
Anxiety about anything is forbidden as a stance of the inner person; prayer and thanksgiving are given as the redirecting action that displaces anxious worry with trust.
set.keywords: ["anxiety forbidden", "worry displaced", "prayer redirecting", "trust replacing", "inner stance", "will redirected"]

set.analysis_note: Parental provocation can crush the child's spirit into discouragement — an externally induced inner deflation that collapses motivation and confidence.
The fainthearted are identified as a distinct inner-need group whose smallness of soul requires encouragement — their diminished courage calls for targeted pastoral support.
The will to persist in doing good is liable to fatigue; growing weary is named as the inner drift to be resisted so that good action is not abandoned.
set.keywords: ["weariness moral", "will persisting", "fatigue inner", "good action sustained", "heart steadied"]

set.analysis_note: Double-mindedness produces pervasive inner instability — a divided soul whose wavering between two allegiances makes consistent thought and action impossible.
set.keywords: ["soul divided", "instability pervasive", "allegiance split", "will wavering", "mind double", "inner incoherence"]

set.analysis_note: Double-mindedness is located in the heart and paired with moral impurity; cleansing the heart means resolving the split between loyalty to God and loyalty to the world.
set.keywords: ["heart divided", "soul split", "loyalty conflicted", "purity lacking", "will undivided", "heart cleansing"]

set.analysis_note: Anxieties are real burdens held within the self; the act of casting them on God is the prescribed inner movement that transfers the weight and rests in his care.
set.keywords: ["anxiety burden", "soul releasing", "trust transferring", "care divine", "weight cast", "inner surrender"]
